prompt:
Our lobby kiosk cycles little ASCII demos and I want Conway's Life in the rotation. The catch: the kiosk boxes run hooks with an EMPTY PATH, so this has to be pure bash — no awk, no grep, no sort, nothing that forks an external tool. One script, `lifegame.sh` in the repo root, run as `lifegame.sh <pattern-file> <generations>`.

Pattern files are the same little format our other demos use: lines starting with '!' are comments, blank lines are ignored, every other line is one grid row made of '.' (dead) and '#' (alive). The grid is a small bounded rectangle — everything outside it is permanently dead, no wrapping. Standard Life rules, all updates simultaneous per generation: a live cell survives with exactly 2 or 3 live neighbours (8-neighbourhood), a dead cell is born with exactly 3.

Output: print generation 0 (the parsed pattern, exactly as loaded) through generation N, each frame as a header line `gen K pop P` (P = live-cell count) followed by the grid rows, frames separated by a single blank line. Exit 0.

Be strict about garbage, message on stderr and exit 2 in every case, nothing on stdout: wrong argument count -> `usage: lifegame.sh <pattern-file> <generations>`; unreadable file -> `error: cannot read <path>`; generations not a plain non-negative integer -> `error: generations must be a non-negative integer`; a row character that isn't '.' or '#' -> `error: invalid character: <char>`; rows of different lengths -> `error: ragged pattern`; a file with no grid rows at all -> `error: empty pattern`.

`bash test_lifegame.sh` is the acceptance gate — it pins exact frame sequences for the classic patterns (blinker, toad, a glider sailing into a bounded corner and settling into a block) and it launches the script with PATH='' every single time, so forking anything external fails everything. Don't touch the test file.
